sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 42, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Each action moves the white character 3 cells in that direction. - White char now at rows 14-28, cols 14-28. Red feet at rows 29-31. - Green frame at rows 29-49, cols 41-49 with interior at ~rows 32-46, cols 44-46. - Need to move character toward green frame: ~9 RIGHT and ~5 DOWN from current position.
- Goal model: Move the white character to interact with the green frame (possibly enter it or match its position).
- Action model: UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT likely move the white character. Need to probe.
- Plan: Batch RIGHT moves to approach the green frame horizontally, then DOWN to align vertically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 41 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 1.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The UP action was stopped early because it was marked as `no_impact`. But looking at the image, the character has changed - it now has red blocks on top and green at bottom. Let me check the green count.
Wait, the result says `gameplay_changed: false` and `no_impact: true`. This means the UP action only changed the HUD band, not the actual gameplay. But the image looks different... Let me check the segmentation to understand what actually changed.
Actually, looking more carefully at the result: `executed_count: 1`, `stopped_early: true`, `stop_reason: "no_impact_action"`. So only UP was executed, and it was considered no_impact. The SPACE wasn't executed.
Let me check the green count to see if anything changed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
greens = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N']
print(f"Green total: {sum(n['pixels'] for n in greens)} in {len(greens)} pieces")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'S':
        minr = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxr = max(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        minc = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        maxc = max(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        print(f"id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} rows={minr}-{maxr} cols={minc}-{maxc}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Green went from 45 to 54. The UP action increased green by 9. So the no_impact flag was wrong about gameplay - green DID change. But the system considers it no_impact because it only changed the HUD band... wait, no. The result said `gameplay_changed: false` and `no_impact: true`. But green changed from 45 to 54. This is confusing.
Let me reconsider the whole approach. I've been going in circles with green oscillating between different values. Maybe the goal isn't to reduce green to 0.
Looking at the image more carefully, I see:
- Red blocks at top (29-31)
- White character body (32-46)
- Red block on left (35-37)
- Green pieces inside and below
- Green bar at bottom (47-49)
The character seems to be a "container" that holds green pieces. When I move it, the green pieces shift relative to it. When I press SPACE, the character rotates/flips.
Wait, let me look at the history of green counts more carefully:
144 -> 135 -> 99 -> 54 -> 90 -> 99 -> 72 -> 90 -> 45 -> 90 -> 72 -> 108 -> 54 -> 90 -> 45 -> 54
The minimum I've seen is 45. Maybe 45 is the target? Or maybe I need to get to 0?
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. The green pieces seem to be "inside" or "overlapping" with the character. When the character moves, some green pieces become covered (and disappear from segmentation as green) and others become exposed.
Wait, I think I've been misunderstanding the game. Let me look at the segmentation more carefully. The green pieces are separate objects from the character. When the character overlaps with green, the green is still visible as separate objects.
Actually, looking at the current state:
- Green: 54 pixels in 4 pieces
- Character (white): rows 32-46, cols 38-52
- Red blocks: rows 29-31 (top), rows 35-37 (left)
The green pieces are:
- rows 29-31, cols 44-46 (9px) - between red blocks at top
- rows 41-43, cols 41-43 (9px) - inside character
- rows 41-43, cols 47-49 (9px) - inside character
- rows 47-49, cols 41-49 (27px) - below character
Hmm, the green at rows 29-31 is between the red blocks. The green at rows 41-43 is inside the character. The green at rows 47-49 is below.
I think the goal might be to have the character "collect" all the green pieces by overlapping them, then pressing SPACE to "consume" them. But the SPACE seems to toggle between states.
Let me try a different approach. Instead of moving randomly, let me try to systematically cover all green pieces with the character, then press SPACE.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the pattern of successful green reductions:
- 90 -> 45 (SPACE after some moves)
- 108 -> 54 (DOWN, DOWN, SPACE)
- 90 -> 45 (DOWN, SPACE)
In all these cases, the character was moved to a new position, then SPACE was pressed. The green reduced.
But then:
- 45 -> 90 (SPACE alone)
- 54 -> 90 (SPACE alone)
- 45 -> 54 (UP alone)
When SPACE is pressed without moving, or when the character moves in the wrong direction, green increases.
So the pattern is: move character to a NEW position that overlaps with green, then SPACE reduces green. But if I press SPACE again without moving, green increases.
Let me try: move character DOWN to cover the bottom green (47-49), then SPACE.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green went from 45 to 54 after UP - UP increased green
# Current char: rows 32-46, cols 38-52
# Green: top (29-31, 9px), middle (41-43, 18px), bottom (47-49, 27px)
# Total: 54
# Let me try DOWN to cover bottom green, then SPACE
# Use intent='solve' to bypass no_impact check
action(['DOWN','SPACE'], intent='solve')
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
